model_narrative: We plan production of several products over a sequence of time periods, drawing on a shared pool of raw materials. In each active period we decide how many units of each product to make, and we track how much of each raw material remains in inventory from one period to the next. Selling a product in a period earns a known per-unit profit, holding raw material in inventory incurs a per-unit storage charge each period, and any raw material left over at the very end carries a residual worth that may be positive or negative. The objective is to maximize the total profit over the whole horizon.

sets.name: p
sets.doc: the products that can be manufactured

sets.name: r
sets.doc: the raw materials consumed in manufacturing

sets.name: tl
sets.doc: the extended set of time periods in chronological order; this runs one period longer than the active production periods so that the inventory carried out of the last production period can still be valued at the end

sets.name: t
sets.doc: the active production periods, a subset of the extended periods consisting of every extended period except the final one; production and sales only happen in these periods

params.name: b
params.doc: the initial on-hand stock of each raw material at the very start of the horizon, in units

params.name: d
params.doc: the storage cost charged for holding one unit of a raw material through one active period, in dollars per unit per period

params.name: f
params.doc: the residual worth of one unit of a raw material left over at the very end of the horizon, in dollars per unit; this can be negative when leftover material is a disposal liability rather than an asset

params.name: a
params.doc: the amount of each raw material required to make one unit of each product, in units of raw material per unit of product

params.name: c
params.doc: the profit earned from selling one unit of a product in a given active period, in dollars per unit

params.name: m
params.doc: the maximum total number of product units that can be produced in any single active period, in units

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the production level of each product in each extended period; production in the final extended period is held at zero because that period exists only to value carried-out inventory

vars.name: s
vars.doc: the inventory of each raw material on hand at the beginning of each extended period; the beginning inventory of the first period is the initial stock, and the beginning inventory of the final extended period is the material left over at the end of the horizon

vars.name: profit
vars.doc: the total profit over the whole horizon, in dollars

description: Production capacity is limited in each active production period. For every active period, the total number of product units made across all products that period must not exceed the per-period production limit.

Selling a product in a period earns a known per-unit profit, holding raw material in inventory incurs a per-unit storage charge each period, and any raw material left over at the very end carries a residual worth that may be positive or negative. The objective is to maximize the total profit over the whole horizon.

description: Raw material inventory carries forward from one period to the next as it gets consumed by production. For each raw material and each period that is followed by another period, the inventory on hand at the start of the next period equals the inventory on hand at the start of the current period minus the total amount of that raw material consumed by all production in the current period. The amount of a raw material consumed by making a product is the per-unit requirement times the units produced, summed over all products. There is no such balance written out of the final period since no period follows it.

In each active period we decide how many units of each product to make, and we track how much of each raw material remains in inventory from one period to the next. Selling a product in a period earns a known per-unit profit, holding raw material in inventory incurs a per-unit storage charge each period, and any raw material left over at the very end carries a residual worth that may be positive or negative. The objective is to maximize the total profit over the whole horizon.

vars.doc: the total profit over the whole horizon, in dollars

description: Total profit ties together sales income, storage charges, and the worth of leftover material. Start from the sales income, which values each product made and sold in an active period at that product's per-unit profit for that period, summed over all products and all active periods. From this subtract the storage charges, which apply the per-unit storage cost of each raw material to the inventory of that material held at the start of each active period. Then add the end-of-horizon worth, which values the inventory of each raw material left over at the start of the final extended period at that material's residual worth. Set the total profit equal to this combined amount.
expected_pyomo:
def profit_def_rule(model):
    last_tl = max(model.tl.data())
    revenue = sum(model.c[p, t] * model.x[p, t] for p in model.p for t in model.t)
    inv_term = sum(
        ((-model.d[r] if tl in model.t else 0.0) + (model.f[r] if tl == last_tl else 0.0)) * model.s[r, tl]
        for r in model.r for tl in model.tl)
    return model.profit == revenue + inv_term
model.profit_def = Constraint(rule=profit_def_rule)

In each active period we decide how many units of each product to make, and we track how much of each raw material remains in inventory from one period to the next. Selling a product in a period earns a known per-unit profit, holding raw material in inventory incurs a per-unit storage charge each period, and any raw material left over at the very end carries a residual worth that may be positive or negative. The objective is to maximize the total profit over the whole horizon.

description: To build the complete model, enforce the following relationships in order. First, production capacity is limited in each active production period. For every active period, the total number of product units made across all products that period must not exceed the per-period production limit. Second, raw material inventory carries forward from one period to the next as it gets consumed by production. For each raw material and each period that is followed by another period, the inventory on hand at the start of the next period equals the inventory on hand at the start of the current period minus the total amount of that raw material consumed by all production in the current period. The amount of a raw material consumed by making a product is the per-unit requirement times the units produced, summed over all products. There is no such balance written out of the final period since no period follows it. Finally, total profit ties together sales income, storage charges, and the worth of leftover material. Start from the sales income, which values each product made and sold in an active period at that product's per-unit profit for that period, summed over all products and all active periods. From this subtract the storage charges, which apply the per-unit storage cost of each raw material to the inventory of that material held at the start of each active period. Then add the end-of-horizon worth, which values the inventory of each raw material left over at the start of the final extended period at that material's residual worth. Set the total profit equal to this combined amount.
